topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed, a high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly behind my eyes. My hands were slick with sweat as I reread the accusation, the printed words blurring into an illegible smear. Every time I looked at the professor’s stern, unyielding face, a cold knot tightened in my stomach, making it hard to draw a full breath. I kept shifting in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, desperate for any sensation other than the heavy pressure building behind my ribs. *It can’t be true. I didn't copy anything. I worked for weeks.* The silence of the study room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I traced the outline of the highlighted paragraph on my screen, my fingers trembling so violently I had to stop, pressing my palms flat against the desk to steady myself.

[story 2]

"Are you certain about this?" The words felt like shards of glass in my mouth. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the effort painful. I avoided meeting Dr. Albright’s gaze, focusing instead on the dust motes dancing in the single shaft of afternoon sunlight cutting across the mahogany desk. My jaw was locked so tight I could feel the strain in my molars. Each rustle of paper in the adjacent stacks sounded like an accusation. I kept smoothing down my already perfectly straight shirt, a futile attempt to smooth out the internal disarray. My heart hammered against my sternum, a frantic, trapped bird demanding release. I felt a sudden, dizzying need to flee the room, to just disappear into the crowded hallway, anywhere but this spotlight.

[story 3]

I kept replaying the moment I submitted the paper, searching for a slip-up, a misplaced phrase that could be twisted into evidence. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, each inhale catching painfully in my chest. The air in the small dorm room felt thick, suffocating. I paced three steps to the window and back, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. Outside, the campus seemed too bright, too loud, mocking the internal turmoil raging within me. I picked up my phone, intending to call a friend, but my thumb hovered over the dial button, paralyzed by the sheer weight of the impending consequence. My vision tunneled slightly at the edges, and I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the racing thoughts to slow down, to settle into some semblance of order.
